title: Buprenorphine For Opioid Use Disorder
author: Ned

seo.title: Buprenorphine For Opioid Use Disorder | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Buprenorphine For Opioid Use Disorder. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
Therapeutic Effects

Buprenorphine is a partial agonist. It activates the brain’s opioid receptors but it also binds to and blocks the effect of opioid agonists such as heroin and methadone. This partial antagonism means that buprenorphine cannot be combined with other opioids to get high.

Buprenorphine also manifests a ceiling effect at which additional buprenorphine causes no additional respiratory depression. This ceiling makes buprenorphine safer than heroin, methadone, and other prescription opioids which have no protective ceiling. Buprenorphine can nevertheless contribute to fatal respiratory depression when combined with other respiratory depressants.

Formulations

Buprenorphine is available in two formulations, Subutex® and Suboxone®, which are administered sublingually as tablets or films. These come in 2mg and 8mg formulations.

  • Subutex® is a single entity buprenorphine product. It is less expensive that Suboxone® and is now available generically.
  • Suboxone® combines buprenorphine with naloxone in a 4:1 ratio. Naloxone is a brief-acting opioid antagonist which is used in emergency rooms to reverse the effects of an opioid overdose. In Suboxone®, naloxone is generally inert, but it becomes active if a person attempts to inject the medication rather than taking it as prescribed. Instead of getting high, a person who injects Suboxone® will precipitate opioid withdrawal. Suboxone® is used in place of Subutex® with clients who have a history of IV substance use.

Induction and Use

Buprenorphine induction generally takes place once an individual is experiencing mild to moderate withdrawal symptoms (approximately 12-24 hours after last use of heroin or other short-acting opioids). If induction takes place too early, buprenorphine will precipitate withdrawal symptoms. SAMHSA guidelines specify 8mg as the maximum Day 1 dose, after which the dose can be gradually increased until a therapeutic dose is reached. Most persons stabilize on 16-24mg. The maximum therapeutic dose is 32mg.

Availability

Suboxone® and Subutex® were FDA approved in 2002. Because of their ceiling effect for respiratory depression, certified physicians are permitted to prescribe Suboxone® and Subutex® in a regular doctor’s office for self-administration at home. This procedure eliminates the need for daily clinic trips and reduces the stigma associated with Medication Assisted Treatment. By the end of 2010, there were nearly 500,000 persons prescribed buprenorphine, far surpassing the number of persons receiving methadone treatment.

Unless an individual has insurance and finds a certified buprenorphine physician who accepts it, buprenorphine treatment can cost several hundred dollars each month. Since 2003, federally certified Opioid Treatment Programs (OTPs) that administer methadone have been authorized to administer buprenorphine products for detoxification and maintenance. As of 2008, 43.2% of OTPs offered buprenorphine treatment in addition to methadone treatment, but only a small fraction of persons served by OTPs actually receive buprenorphine treatment. Persons typically seek buprenorphine through private physicians who can prescribe buprenorphine for at-home administration.

Overdose Risk

By itself, buprenorphine has a ceiling effect for respiratory depression, making it much less likely that a person will overdose on buprenorphine than on other opioids. However, taken together with other central nervous system depressants, buprenorphine can cause fatal respiratory depression. Between 2006 and 2010 the national number of emergency room visits associated with buprenorphine nearly quadrupled to 19,628.

Medically Supervised Withdrawal

SAMHSA’s guidelines for medically supervised withdrawal from buprenorphine are similar to those for methadone: “Maintenance can be relatively short‐term (e.g., <12 months) or a lifetime process. Treatment success depends on the achievement of specific goals that are agreed on by both the patient and the physician. Following successful stabilization, decisions to decrease or discontinue buprenorphine should be based on a patient’s desires and commitment to becoming medication‐free, and on the physician’s confidence that tapering would be successful.” As with methadone, a patient’s commitment to abstinence and stability in medical and mental health, employment, legal status, and primary support system should be assessed prior to the initiation of medically supervised withdrawal.

12-Step Compatibility

Because buprenorphine is dependence-producing, persons taking buprenorphine are often poorly received in 12-step programs. Persons on buprenorphine maintenance are likely to receive more support at Methadone Anonymous meetings.
